title: The Synergistic Route to Stretched Criticality
authors: Lorenzo Lucarini, Sandro Meloni, Pablo Villegas
abstract: Griffiths phases are typically associated with quenched disorder, while frustration gives rise to multistability and spin-glass behavior. Whether extended criticality can arise in other contexts remains an open question. Here, we show that synergistic interactions provide a distinct route to non-conventional critical phenomena. By combining spreading mechanisms that reinforce activity through complementary pathways, we uncover a broad distribution of relaxation rates, leading to Griffiths-like slow dynamics and extended criticality. We demonstrate that this mechanism is robust across networks and emerges both in systems with explicit higher-order interactions and in purely pairwise systems with nonlinear dynamics.
summary: The paper identifies 'synergistic interactions' as a new way to generate extended critical regimes and slow, Griffiths-like dynamics. By coupling different spreading mechanisms, the system develops a broad spectrum of relaxation rates. This effect is shown to be a general dynamical principle that works across various network structures and interaction types.
main_problem: Investigating whether synergistic interactions (cooperative mechanisms) can serve as a distinct route to non-conventional critical phenomena, specifically 'stretched criticality' characterized by extended regimes of slow, algebraic-like decay.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that synergistic interactions create a broad distribution of relaxation rates, leading to Griffiths-like slow dynamics and an extended critical region that is robust across different network topologies and interaction types.
method: The study uses spectral analysis of the diffusion density operator (including von Neumann entropy and spectral specific heat) and numerical simulations of SIS-type processes on various networks.
model_or_system: The primary models are a simplicial Susceptible-Infected-Susceptible (SIS) process on a 2D triangular lattice and a pairwise quadratic contact process with threshold activation, applied to Erdős–Rényi, scientific collaboration, and US airport networks.
key_observables: Infection density rho(t), relaxation rates, von Neumann entropy S(tau), spectral specific heat C(tau), and integrated spectral excess Phi.
important_parameters: Cooperative fraction p (density of active 2-simplices), synergistic strength (ratio of beta_delta to beta), and infection/recovery rates.
main_assumptions: The mechanism is assumed to be independent of specific geometry, though finite-size effects in heterogeneous networks like the US airport network may limit perfectly clean power-law behavior.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows the temporal evolution of infection density highlighting the extended slow relaxation regime; Figure 3 demonstrates the robustness of this effect across different network topologies; Supplemental figures show how varying p and beta_delta/beta affects the presence of stretched criticality.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of stretched criticality, presents the simplicial and pairwise models, details the spectral analysis method, demonstrates the emergence of slow dynamics through synergy, tests robustness across networks, and concludes with the conditions (balance of strength and density) required for the phenomenon.
why_interesting: This is relevant for many-body dynamics and open quantum systems as it identifies a dynamical mechanism for generating broad relaxation spectra and extended critical regimes, which could be applied to understanding non-equilibrium phase transitions in driven-dissipative systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Partial activation of cooperative units generates additional slow relaxation modes. (a) Triangular lattice with an active fraction p of 2-simplices (triangles, teal) superimposed on the pairwise backbone. Node colors represent SIS states: susceptible (white) and infected (red). (b) Spreading dynamics combining pairwise infection (β), cooperative simplicial infection (β∆), and recovery (µ). (c),(d) Spectral specific heat C as a function of normalized diffusion time τ/τ ∗for two values of the active simplicial fraction, p = 0.05 and p = 0.4, for N = 1023. Relative to the pairwise baseline (p = 0, red), partial activation of simplices generates an additional peak at short times,...

Figure 2. Extended region of slow relaxation induced by synergistic interactions. Temporal evolution of the in- fection density ρ(t) for two values of the cooperative fraction, p = 0.05 and p = 0.25. (a,b) Simplicial SIS dynamics: (a) β ∈(0.220, 0.238) with βc = 0.233; (b) β ∈(0.150, 0.165) with βlow c ≡βl c = 0.152 and βhigh c ≡βh c = 0.163. (c,d) Pairwise quadratic contact process: (c) β ∈(0.220, 0.242) with βc = 0.240; (d) β ∈(0.140, 0.184) with βl c = 0.152 and βh c = 0.180. In both models, the system develops a broad region of algebraic-like decay over several decades (green curves), signaling the emergence of an extended crit- ical regime associated with a broad spectrum of relaxation...

Figure 3. Robustness across network structures. Temporal evolution of the infection density ρ(t) for the pairwise cooperative model on different network topologies for strong synergy and absent synergy (insets, p = 0). Blue curves correspond to the absorbing phase and red curves to the active one, while the greenish ones highlight algebraic decay. (a) Erdős–Rényi network (N = 104, ⟨κ⟩= 3, p = 0.10), with β ∈(0.31, 0.35), βl c = 0.32, and βh c = 0.33. Inset: β ∈(0.30, 0.40), with βc = 0.340. (b) Scientific collaboration network (Netsci, N = 379, p = 0.30), with β ∈(0.10, 0.20), βl c = 0.13, and βh c = 0.17. Inset: β ∈(0.10, 0.28), with βc = 0.22. (c) US airport network (N = 500, p = 0.20),...
